use_when: Child, infant older than 7 days, or adolescent with fever, suspected infection, or possible sepsis.
Pediatric or adolescent fever workup needs age-band routing, source localization, and escalation criteria.
do_not_use_when: Adult patient; use the adult fever, infection, or sepsis workup instead.
Neonate younger than 8 days or unstable newborn; use local neonatal sepsis pathway and urgent pediatric/neonatal escalation.
Pregnant or recently pregnant adolescent; use pregnancy or postpartum sepsis guidance with obstetric escalation.

differentialBuckets.label: Pediatric sepsis or serious bacterial infection with organ dysfunction, shock physiology, altered responsiveness, hypoxemia, oliguria, meningitis signs, or rapid worsening
differentialBuckets.action: Escalate early, obtain age-appropriate sepsis severity data, cultures when they will not substantially delay antibiotics, lactate when available, and source-directed testing.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Pediatric sepsis is defined by infection with life-threatening organ dysfunction; early recognition and treatment timing change mortality risk and level of care.

source.source_section: pediatric sepsis definition, screening, lactate, cultures, antimicrobials, fluids, and hemodynamic monitoring
source.implementation_notes: Use pediatric age-adjusted thresholds and local pediatric sepsis pathway; do not infer adult thresholds for children.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Pediatric sepsis or serious bacterial infection with organ dysfunction, shock physiology, altered responsiveness, hypoxemia, oliguria, meningitis signs, or rapid worsening.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Escalate early, obtain age-appropriate sepsis severity data, cultures when they will not substantially delay antibiotics, lactate when available, and source-directed testing.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not applicable to this diagnostic-frame row; use guideline-defined organ dysfunction and bedside physiology rather than adult sepsis heuristics.
differentialBuckets.limitations: This does not replace institutional pediatric sepsis activation criteria, age-specific vital-sign charts, or neonatal sepsis pathways.

differentialBuckets.label: Respiratory infection source such as pneumonia, bronchiolitis, viral respiratory infection, aspiration, or hypoxemic respiratory illness
differentialBuckets.action: Ask cough, breathing, apnea, exposure, and oxygen-need questions; use work-of-breathing observation, pulse oximetry, and lung auscultation to decide imaging, respiratory testing, isolation, and escalation.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Respiratory source and respiratory compromise are common management-changing causes of pediatric fever and suspected sepsis.

source.source_section: recognition of suspected sepsis in children and young people and initial assessment domains
source.implementation_notes: Pair respiratory symptoms with respiratory rate, oxygen saturation, work of breathing, and source-directed testing.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Respiratory infection source such as pneumonia, bronchiolitis, viral respiratory infection, aspiration, or hypoxemic respiratory illness.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Respiratory findings change oxygen, chest imaging, viral testing, isolation, antibiotics, and pediatric escalation.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not attached to this differential bucket; apply source-specific pediatric evidence and guideline pathways when testing is selected.
differentialBuckets.limitations: A normal lung exam does not exclude early pneumonia, bronchiolitis, or hypoxemia; use oxygen saturation and trajectory.

differentialBuckets.label: Urinary, abdominal, skin, line, CNS, joint, travel, exposure, or immunization-related fever source
differentialBuckets.action: Use source-localizing history to decide urine testing, abdominal evaluation, skin/line inspection, meningitis assessment, joint evaluation, travel/exposure testing, and immunization-sensitive risk review.
differentialBuckets.rationale: Pediatric fever workups should actively localize infection source without importing unrelated adult organ-system maneuvers.

source.source_section: fever without obvious cause assessment, traffic-light risk features, and safety-netting
source.implementation_notes: Use the exact age band and source symptoms to select pediatric testing and escalation.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Urinary, abdominal, skin, line, CNS, joint, travel, exposure, or immunization-related fever source.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Source localization changes urine, respiratory, skin, CSF, abdominal, and exposure-directed testing plus antimicrobial and disposition decisions.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not attached to this broad source bucket; use source-specific testing and guideline rationale once a source is suspected.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Fever source may remain unclear initially; persistent or worsening symptoms need reassessment rather than reassurance from a single negative screen.

differentialBuckets.label: Well-appearing febrile infant 8 to 60 days with rectal temperature at least 38.0 C and gestational age 37 to 42 weeks
differentialBuckets.action: Confirm eligibility before applying febrile-infant algorithms; if not eligible or ill-appearing, escalate to local pediatric or neonatal sepsis pathway.
differentialBuckets.rationale: The AAP febrile infant pathway is age- and eligibility-specific; applying it outside scope can under-triage unstable or excluded infants.

source.source_section: eligibility and age-stratified evaluation for well-appearing febrile infants 8 to 60 days
source.implementation_notes: This module only routes eligibility and safety cues; use local pediatric protocols for exact lab bundles and disposition.

differentialBuckets.diagnostic_target: Well-appearing febrile infant 8 to 60 days with rectal temperature at least 38.0 C and gestational age 37 to 42 weeks.
differentialBuckets.management_change: Eligibility changes urine, blood, inflammatory-marker, CSF, antimicrobial, observation, and discharge decisions.
differentialBuckets.likelihood_ratio_note: Likelihood ratios are not applicable to pathway eligibility; use the AAP age and inclusion criteria before selecting the infant workup.
differentialBuckets.limitations: Do not use this eligibility row for neonates younger than 8 days, premature infants outside guideline scope, ill-appearing infants, or infants with focal bacterial infection requiring direct treatment.

safetyChecks.label: Measure temperature
safetyChecks.action: Measure and document temperature with method; confirm fever threshold and trend with age-appropriate interpretation.
safetyChecks.rationale: Temperature confirms fever, anchors age-band pathways, and supports reassessment of response to antipyretics or treatment.

source.source_section: assessment of fever and risk stratification in children under 5
source.implementation_notes: Document method and timing; rectal temperature is part of AAP febrile-infant eligibility.

safetyChecks.management_change: Temperature level, method, trend, and age band determine febrile infant routing, safety-netting, and escalation urgency.
safetyChecks.limitations: Temperature alone does not grade sepsis severity; combine with mental status, perfusion, respiratory status, and source findings.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to temperature as baseline clinical data; management depends on age band, source, and physiologic status.

safetyChecks.label: Measure respiratory rate
safetyChecks.action: Count respiratory rate for a full minute when feasible and compare with age-adjusted concern thresholds.
safetyChecks.rationale: Respiratory rate helps identify respiratory source, hypoxemic illness, shock physiology, and pediatric sepsis risk.

source.source_section: initial assessment and age-adjusted physiologic risk features
source.implementation_notes: Use local age-specific charts rather than adult tachypnea thresholds.

safetyChecks.management_change: Tachypnea for age changes respiratory workup, oxygen need, sepsis concern, and level-of-care decisions.
safetyChecks.limitations: Crying, fever, pain, and agitation can elevate rate; reassess after calming and trend with oxygen saturation and work of breathing.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to respiratory rate as baseline clinical data; use age-adjusted guideline thresholds and clinical trajectory.

safetyChecks.label: Measure oxygen saturation
safetyChecks.action: Measure oxygen saturation and document oxygen device or room air context.
safetyChecks.rationale: Hypoxemia changes respiratory source evaluation, oxygen treatment, sepsis risk, and urgency of pediatric escalation.

source.source_section: recognition of suspected sepsis and respiratory compromise in children and young people
source.implementation_notes: Record device, flow, probe quality, and trend.

safetyChecks.management_change: Low oxygen saturation prompts oxygen, respiratory imaging/testing, continuous monitoring, and escalation.
safetyChecks.limitations: Poor perfusion, motion, nail products, or probe fit can distort readings; verify when discordant with clinical appearance.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to oxygen saturation as baseline safety data; management is threshold- and context-driven.

safetyChecks.label: Measure heart rate
safetyChecks.action: Measure heart rate and compare with age-adjusted concern thresholds and fever/pain context.
safetyChecks.rationale: Persistent tachycardia for age can reflect fever, dehydration, shock, sepsis, pain, or respiratory distress.

source.source_section: hemodynamic monitoring markers including heart rate, perfusion, mental status, and urine output
source.implementation_notes: Trend after antipyretic, fluids, analgesia, and respiratory support when clinically appropriate.

safetyChecks.management_change: Persistent tachycardia for age changes perfusion assessment, fluid reassessment, sepsis concern, and disposition.
safetyChecks.limitations: Fever, crying, pain, dehydration, medications, and baseline conditions affect rate; interpret with perfusion and trajectory.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to heart rate as baseline safety data; use age-adjusted guideline thresholds and clinical trend.

safetyChecks.label: Measure blood pressure
safetyChecks.action: Measure blood pressure with correct cuff size and compare with age-adjusted hypotension concern thresholds.
safetyChecks.rationale: Hypotension is a late and dangerous pediatric shock sign and changes sepsis activation, fluids, vasopressor consideration, and monitored setting.

source.source_section: hemodynamic markers and fluid resuscitation recommendations
source.implementation_notes: Do not wait for hypotension if perfusion, mental status, urine output, or respiratory status suggests shock.

safetyChecks.management_change: Hypotension for age triggers urgent pediatric sepsis escalation, fluid/vasopressor pathway, and higher-acuity monitoring.
safetyChecks.limitations: Incorrect cuff size, agitation, and motion can distort blood pressure; normal blood pressure does not exclude compensated pediatric shock.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to blood pressure as baseline safety data; pediatric shock assessment depends on age-adjusted thresholds and perfusion context.

safetyChecks.label: Document mental status
safetyChecks.action: Document alertness, interaction, consolability, responsiveness, seizure activity, and caregiver comparison with baseline.
safetyChecks.rationale: Altered responsiveness is a high-risk pediatric fever and sepsis feature that changes escalation and neurologic or meningitis evaluation.

source.source_section: recognition of suspected sepsis and neurologic risk features in children and young people
source.implementation_notes: Record caregiver report because children may look different from their baseline even with subtle exam changes.

safetyChecks.management_change: Altered responsiveness, seizure, or caregiver report of serious change prompts urgent escalation and CNS/sepsis pathway.
safetyChecks.limitations: Sleep, antipyretics, medications, baseline neurodevelopment, and caregiver context affect interpretation.
safetyChecks.likelihood_ratio_note: LR is not applicable to mental status as baseline acuity data; action is driven by danger-pattern recognition.

requiredQuestions.label: Exact pediatric age band
requiredQuestions.text: What is the child exact age band: 8 to 60 days, 2 to 5 months, under 5 years, 5 to 15 years, or 16 years and older?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask at the start of every pediatric fever or suspected sepsis workup.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Determines whether AAP febrile-infant, NICE under-5 fever, NICE under-16 sepsis, adolescent, or adult pathway constraints apply.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Age band changes fever threshold interpretation, urine/blood/CSF testing, antibiotic timing, observation, discharge criteria, and escalation pathway.

source.source_section: age-stratified febrile infant eligibility and management
source.implementation_notes: Pair with NICE pediatric fever/sepsis guidance outside the 8 to 60 day well-appearing infant scope.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available because age band is a pathway-eligibility variable and management changes by guideline scope.

requiredQuestions.label: Pediatric fever severity cues
requiredQuestions.text: Which severity cues are endorsed or denied: poor feeding, reduced responsiveness, respiratory distress, persistent vomiting, seizure, non-blanching rash, weak cry, reduced urine output, caregiver concern, or rapid worsening?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask in every pediatric fever workup and document positives and negatives separately.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Screens for sepsis, meningitis, dehydration, respiratory failure, and serious illness danger patterns.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Any endorsed danger cue should move the child toward urgent pediatric reassessment, sepsis pathway, ED/PICU escalation, or source-directed urgent testing.

source.source_section: recognition of suspected sepsis in children and young people
source.implementation_notes: Keep this as a multi-select symptom-control row so denied and endorsed symptoms are visible.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this multi-select danger-cue row across pediatric age bands; use guideline danger features and trajectory.

requiredQuestions.label: Respiratory infection-source symptoms
requiredQuestions.text: Are cough, fast breathing, noisy breathing, apnea, chest pain, oxygen need, known respiratory exposure, or difficulty feeding because of breathing endorsed or denied?
options: endorsed difficulty feeding because of breathing
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask before deciding whether respiratory source imaging, respiratory testing, oxygen, isolation, or escalation is needed.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Screens for pediatric respiratory source, pneumonia, bronchiolitis, hypoxemic illness, apnea, and respiratory sepsis features.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive respiratory source symptoms change oxygen monitoring, work-of-breathing assessment, lung auscultation priority, respiratory testing, chest imaging, isolation, antibiotics, and escalation.

source.source_section: respiratory concern and recognition of suspected sepsis in children and young people
source.implementation_notes: Keep respiratory source history visible because it changes cardiopulmonary exam, oxygen, imaging, and sepsis routing.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this respiratory source-localizing row; use guideline rationale and source-specific testing when indicated.

requiredQuestions.label: Pediatric fever source-localizing symptoms
requiredQuestions.text: Which fever source symptoms are endorsed or denied: cough or breathing symptoms, ear or throat symptoms, dysuria or reduced urine, abdominal pain, rash or skin infection, line infection, neck stiffness, joint swelling, travel or exposure, immunocompromise, or incomplete immunization?
requiredQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask before selecting imaging, cultures, urine testing, respiratory testing, or source-specific exam add-ons.
requiredQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Localizes likely infection source and prevents generic fever workups from missing urinary, respiratory, skin, CNS, abdominal, or exposure-related sources.
requiredQuestions.management_implication: Positive source cues change tests, antimicrobials, isolation, imaging, consultation, and disposition; explicitly denied cues make the audit trail clearer.

source.source_section: fever without obvious cause assessment, symptoms and signs, and traffic-light system
source.implementation_notes: Use source symptoms to activate focused pediatric exam and testing rather than a broad nonspecific exam bundle.

requiredQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not available for this source-localizing multi-select row; cite source-specific test evidence when a specific diagnostic test is selected.

conditionalQuestions.label: Febrile infant 8 to 60 day eligibility
conditionalQuestions.text: For a well-appearing infant 8 to 60 days old, was rectal temperature at least 38.0 C or 100.4 F and was gestational age 37 to 42 weeks with discharge home after birth?
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask only when age is 8 to 60 days or the child may be in the febrile-infant pathway.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Determines whether the AAP well-appearing febrile infant pathway can be applied or whether local neonatal/pediatric sepsis escalation is needed.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Eligibility affects urine, blood, inflammatory marker, CSF, antimicrobial, observation, discharge, and follow-up decisions.

source.source_section: scope and eligibility criteria for well-appearing febrile infants 8 to 60 days
source.implementation_notes: This item does not implement every age-stratified AAP action statement; it prevents unsafe application outside scope.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable to guideline eligibility criteria for the febrile-infant pathway.

conditionalQuestions.label: Adolescent pregnancy context
conditionalQuestions.text: For an adolescent, is pregnancy possible, currently present, or recent postpartum status possible?
options: yes postpartum or recently pregnant possible
conditionalQuestions.when_to_ask: Ask for adolescents when pregnancy status could change fever, sepsis, VTE, medication, imaging, or disposition decisions.
conditionalQuestions.diagnostic_purpose: Identifies when pediatric sepsis guidance may be insufficient because pregnancy or recent pregnancy requires obstetric or maternal safety routing.
conditionalQuestions.management_implication: Pregnancy or postpartum context should trigger obstetric escalation, medication/imaging safety review, and pregnancy-specific or maternal sepsis guidance.

source.source_section: guideline scope exclusions and initial recognition scope for under-16 suspected sepsis
source.implementation_notes: NICE under-16 suspected sepsis guidance excludes pregnant or recently pregnant patients; route to pregnancy/postpartum pathway when applicable.

conditionalQuestions.likelihood_ratio_note: Question-level LR+/LR- is not applicable because pregnancy context is an applicability and safety-routing variable.

requiredExam.label: Observe work of breathing
requiredExam.technique: Watch chest wall movement, nasal flaring, grunting, retractions, accessory muscle use, apnea, and ability to speak or feed while minimizing distress.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in every pediatric fever or suspected sepsis workup before deciding respiratory source and oxygen escalation.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Respiratory distress, pneumonia, bronchiolitis, hypoxemia, shock physiology, or respiratory failure in a febrile child.

source.source_section: respiratory risk features and initial assessment in suspected sepsis under 16
source.implementation_notes: Document this separately from respiratory rate and oxygen saturation, which are baseline safety data.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available across pediatric age bands for work-of-breathing observation; use it as guideline-supported severity and source evidence.
requiredExam.management_change: Abnormal work of breathing changes oxygen, respiratory testing, chest imaging, sepsis concern, and pediatric escalation.
requiredExam.limitations: Crying, anxiety, fever, bronchodilator response, and baseline respiratory disease can affect interpretation.

requiredExam.label: Inspect skin rash
requiredExam.technique: Expose enough skin to inspect rash morphology, non-blanching lesions, mottling, cellulitis, wounds, indwelling lines, and perfusion color while preserving comfort and privacy.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform in every pediatric fever workup, especially when rash, line, wound, sepsis, meningococcal disease, or cellulitis is possible.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Non-blanching rash, skin or line infection source, poor perfusion, or severe illness skin signs.

source.source_section: traffic-light system and rash or serious illness features in febrile children
source.implementation_notes: Skin inspection is a single exam maneuver; do not bundle it with full HEENT or abdominal exam.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for broad pediatric skin inspection; non-blanching rash is treated as a guideline danger feature requiring escalation.
requiredExam.management_change: Non-blanching rash, purpura, mottling, cellulitis, wound drainage, or line concern changes escalation, cultures, antimicrobials, isolation, and source control.
requiredExam.limitations: Rash may evolve; dark skin, poor lighting, clothing coverage, and partial exam can reduce sensitivity.

requiredExam.label: Capillary refill time
requiredExam.technique: Press a fingertip or central site briefly, release, and time color return while noting temperature, mottling, pulse quality, and ambient factors.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when fever, dehydration, poor intake, tachycardia, altered responsiveness, or suspected sepsis is present.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Poor perfusion, dehydration, compensated shock, or pediatric septic shock physiology.

source.source_section: hemodynamic monitoring markers and reassessment after fluid boluses
source.implementation_notes: Use with heart rate, blood pressure, mental status, urine output, extremity temperature, and pulse quality.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for this knowledge-pack row because capillary refill accuracy varies by setting and age; the pediatric sepsis guideline uses it as a hemodynamic marker.
requiredExam.management_change: Prolonged refill or poor perfusion changes fluid reassessment, sepsis activation, monitoring, and disposition.
requiredExam.limitations: Ambient temperature, lighting, skin pigmentation, peripheral vasoconstriction, and technique affect reliability.

requiredExam.label: Auscultate lung sounds
requiredExam.technique: Auscultate comparable anterior and posterior lung fields when feasible, noting focal crackles, wheeze, diminished breath sounds, asymmetry, and transmitted upper-airway noise.
requiredExam.when_to_perform: Perform when fever could have a respiratory source or oxygen saturation, respiratory rate, cough, work of breathing, or exposure history suggests respiratory illness.
requiredExam.diagnostic_target: Pneumonia, bronchiolitis, asthma/reactive airway component, pleural complication, or respiratory source of fever.

source.source_section: initial assessment and respiratory concern in suspected sepsis under 16
source.implementation_notes: Use with oxygen saturation and work-of-breathing observation to decide imaging and respiratory testing.

requiredExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available for this broad pediatric lung-sound row; abnormal focal findings support source-directed respiratory evaluation.
requiredExam.management_change: Focal or severe lung findings change chest imaging, oxygen, respiratory testing, antimicrobials, and escalation.
requiredExam.limitations: Crying, shallow breathing, upper-airway noise, and early pneumonia can limit sensitivity.

conditionalExam.label: Inspect oropharynx
conditionalExam.technique: Use light and age-appropriate positioning to inspect mucosa, tonsils, exudate, hydration, drooling, swelling, and airway concern without forcing a distressed child.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform when sore throat, poor intake, dehydration, drooling, neck swelling, airway concern, or unclear fever source is present.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: HEENT source of fever, dehydration marker, airway danger, or mucosal findings that change source testing and escalation.

source.source_section: fever assessment and symptoms or signs suggesting serious illness or source
source.implementation_notes: Do not force the maneuver if airway compromise or severe distress is possible; escalate instead.

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available because this row localizes fever source and airway risk rather than diagnosing one condition.
conditionalExam.management_change: Exudate, dehydration, drooling, swelling, or oral lesions change testing, hydration plan, airway escalation, and antimicrobial decisions.
conditionalExam.limitations: Exam may be incomplete in toddlers or distressed children; airway concern should prioritize escalation.

conditionalExam.label: Test passive neck flexion
conditionalExam.technique: When safe and tolerated, gently assess resistance or pain with passive neck flexion while observing irritability, photophobia, bulging fontanelle when applicable, and neurologic change.
conditionalExam.when_to_perform: Perform only when meningitis, CNS infection, neck stiffness, photophobia, seizure, altered responsiveness, or non-blanching rash is suspected and the child is stable enough.
conditionalExam.diagnostic_target: Meningitis, CNS infection, or neurologic danger pattern in a febrile child.

source.source_section: neurologic and meningitis-related danger features in suspected sepsis under 16
source.implementation_notes: Absence of stiffness is not reassuring in young infants; route unstable or concerning cases to urgent pediatric assessment.

conditionalExam.likelihood_ratio_note: No maneuver-specific LR+/LR- is available across pediatric ages; meningitis signs are guideline danger features with important false negatives in infants.
conditionalExam.management_change: Stiffness, photophobia, bulging fontanelle, seizure, or altered responsiveness changes CSF/meningitis evaluation, antimicrobials, and urgent escalation.
conditionalExam.limitations: Young infants may lack neck stiffness; discomfort, crying, trauma concern, or instability can make the maneuver unsafe or unreliable.

initialTests.label: Pediatric sepsis lactate cultures and antibiotic timing
initialTests.action: When sepsis or septic shock is suspected, obtain lactate when available, blood cultures before antimicrobials if this does not substantially delay treatment, and give broad-spectrum antibiotics ideally within 1 hour for shock or within 3 hours for probable sepsis without shock.
initialTests.rationale: Lactate, cultures, and antibiotic timing create the audit trail for severity, pathogen identification, source control, and early treatment.

source.source_section: lactate measurement, blood cultures, and antimicrobial timing recommendations
source.implementation_notes: Do not delay antibiotics for cultures when delay would be clinically substantial.

initialTests.diagnostic_target: Pediatric sepsis severity, bacteremia/pathogen identification, and time-sensitive antimicrobial treatment need.
initialTests.management_change: Positive or worsening severity markers, culture context, and treatment timing change escalation, antibiotic selection, source control, and monitored setting.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: This is a guideline bundle and timing standard, not a standalone diagnostic test with one LR; test interpretation depends on clinical context and source.
initialTests.limitations: Local pediatric sepsis protocols, antibiotic stewardship, allergy history, renal function, source, and available pediatric expertise determine exact orders.

initialTests.label: Age and source-directed pediatric fever tests
initialTests.action: Select source-directed urine testing, respiratory viral testing, chest imaging, CBC or inflammatory markers, CSF evaluation, abdominal tests, skin or wound cultures, or exposure testing based on age band, severity, source symptoms, and local pediatric pathway.
initialTests.rationale: Age and source direct testing prevents both under-testing high-risk children and over-testing low-risk, source-clear fever presentations.

source.source_section: management and investigations for fever without obvious cause in children under 5
source.implementation_notes: Use with NICE under-16 sepsis guidance and AAP febrile-infant pathway when applicable.

initialTests.diagnostic_target: Age-stratified pediatric fever source and serious bacterial infection risk.
initialTests.management_change: Test results change antibiotics, imaging, observation, admission, isolation, follow-up, and source control.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: This row is a testing pathway selector; individual tests require source-specific LR or guideline rationale when used.
initialTests.limitations: This local pack does not encode every age-specific local order set; clinician must apply local pediatric protocols and antimicrobial guidance.

initialTests.label: Febrile infant 8 to 60 day pathway tests
initialTests.action: For eligible well-appearing infants 8 to 60 days with rectal temperature at least 38.0 C, follow local AAP-aligned age-stratified urine, blood, inflammatory-marker, CSF, antimicrobial, observation, and discharge criteria.
initialTests.rationale: Infants 8 to 60 days need a distinct pathway because serious bacterial infection risk, CSF decisions, and discharge safety differ by exact age group.

source.source_section: age-stratified evaluation and management for well-appearing febrile infants 8 to 60 days
source.implementation_notes: This knowledge pack routes to the pathway but does not replace a local pediatric febrile-infant order set.

initialTests.diagnostic_target: Serious bacterial infection risk in a well-appearing febrile infant 8 to 60 days.
initialTests.management_change: Age-stratified tests and inflammatory markers change CSF need, antibiotics, observation, discharge, and follow-up.
initialTests.likelihood_ratio_note: The AAP pathway uses age-stratified criteria and inflammatory markers; this routing row does not have a single LR.
initialTests.limitations: Not for infants younger than 8 days, premature infants outside guideline scope, ill-appearing infants, or infants with focal bacterial infection requiring specific treatment.

redFlags.label: Fever in an infant under 3 months
redFlags.action: Treat as high risk; confirm exact age and temperature method, use pediatric or febrile-infant pathway, and escalate rather than applying routine older-child fever reassurance.
redFlags.rationale: Young infants have higher serious bacterial infection risk and different testing, observation, antibiotic, and discharge criteria.

source.source_section: well-appearing febrile infants 8 to 60 days and age-stratified management
source.implementation_notes: For neonates younger than 8 days or ill-appearing infants, route to local neonatal or pediatric sepsis pathway.

redFlags.management_change: Exact infant age changes tests, CSF consideration, antibiotics, observation, admission, discharge, and follow-up.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: Age is a high-risk pathway criterion, not a diagnostic LR test.
redFlags.limitations: AAP well-appearing infant criteria apply only within eligibility scope; unstable infants need urgent pathway escalation.

redFlags.label: Pediatric sepsis or shock danger physiology
redFlags.action: Escalate urgently for altered responsiveness, seizure, hypoxemia, severe work of breathing, hypotension for age, prolonged capillary refill, mottled or cold extremities, poor pulse quality, oliguria, or rapid deterioration.
redFlags.rationale: Children can compensate before hypotension; danger physiology changes antibiotics, fluids, vasopressors, monitoring, and disposition.

source.source_section: hemodynamic markers, early recognition, antimicrobials, and fluid resuscitation in children
source.implementation_notes: Use age-adjusted thresholds and local pediatric sepsis activation.

redFlags.diagnostic_target: Pediatric sepsis, septic shock, or life-threatening organ dysfunction.
redFlags.management_change: Danger physiology triggers pediatric sepsis pathway, antibiotics, lactate/cultures, fluid reassessment, source control, and ED/PICU escalation.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: No single LR is assigned to this danger cluster; guideline action is driven by physiologic instability and organ dysfunction.
redFlags.limitations: Absence of one feature does not exclude sepsis; trajectory and caregiver concern matter.

redFlags.label: Non-blanching rash meningitis or CNS cues
redFlags.action: Escalate urgently when non-blanching rash, petechiae, purpura, neck stiffness, photophobia, bulging fontanelle, seizure, altered responsiveness, or severe headache is present.
redFlags.rationale: Meningococcal disease, meningitis, and CNS infection can progress quickly and change cultures, CSF evaluation, empiric antimicrobials, isolation, and level of care.

source.source_section: neurologic, rash, and high-risk features in suspected sepsis under 16
source.implementation_notes: Young infants may not show classic meningitis signs.

redFlags.diagnostic_target: Meningococcal disease, meningitis, CNS infection, or severe systemic infection.
redFlags.management_change: These cues change urgency, cultures, CSF decisions, empiric antimicrobials, isolation, monitoring, and disposition.
redFlags.likelihood_ratio_note: This is a danger-feature cluster without a stable cross-age LR; guideline rationale supports urgent action.
redFlags.limitations: Absence of neck stiffness or rash does not exclude meningitis, especially in infants or early disease.

dispositionRules.label: Pediatric sepsis escalation antibiotics and fluids
dispositionRules.action: If septic shock or life-threatening organ dysfunction is suspected, escalate to pediatric sepsis pathway, give broad-spectrum antibiotics ideally within 1 hour, consider 10 to 20 mL/kg isotonic crystalloid boluses with reassessment up to 40 to 60 mL/kg in the first hour where ICU support is available, and reassess after every bolus.
dispositionRules.rationale: Early antibiotics, source control, and carefully reassessed fluid resuscitation change outcomes while avoiding fluid overload in children.

source.source_section: antimicrobial timing, fluid bolus volume, reassessment, and source control recommendations
source.implementation_notes: Use local pediatric sepsis pathway, ICU availability, cardiac disease, malnutrition, and fluid overload risk to adjust fluids.

dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Suspected pediatric sepsis, septic shock, or life-threatening organ dysfunction requiring immediate treatment.
dispositionRules.management_change: Antibiotic timing, fluid bolus volume, reassessment, source control, and ED/PICU escalation become time-sensitive.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: This is a treatment and disposition rule; likelihood ratios are not applicable.
dispositionRules.limitations: Do not apply blindly to all febrile children; use shock/organ dysfunction suspicion, ICU availability, comorbidities, and local pathway.

dispositionRules.label: Pediatric fever safety net and reassessment
dispositionRules.action: For non-toxic children without high-risk features, document source plan, explicit return precautions, caregiver understanding, follow-up timing, and triggers for reassessment if fever persists or symptoms worsen.
dispositionRules.rationale: Clear safety-netting prevents false reassurance when pediatric fever source is uncertain or early serious illness evolves after the first assessment.

source.source_section: safety-netting and management after assessment of fever in children under 5
source.implementation_notes: Include denied and endorsed danger cues in export so follow-up clinicians can audit the disposition decision.

dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Lower-acuity pediatric fever after high-risk features and age-specific pathways have been addressed.
dispositionRules.management_change: Disposition depends on safety-net quality, caregiver reliability, follow-up access, age, source clarity, and symptom trajectory.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: Safety-netting is a disposition process, not a diagnostic test with LR values.
dispositionRules.limitations: Do not use safety-netting to override red flags, concerning caregiver report, unreliable follow-up, or age-specific high-risk pathways.

dispositionRules.label: Pregnant or postpartum adolescent fever route
dispositionRules.action: If an adolescent may be pregnant or recently postpartum, route to pregnancy or postpartum fever/sepsis guidance with obstetric consultation, medication and imaging safety review, and maternal warning-sign handoff.
dispositionRules.rationale: Pregnancy and recent pregnancy change sepsis risk, VTE risk, diagnostic testing, medication safety, source control, and escalation ownership.

source.source_section: scope limitation for pregnant or recently pregnant young people
source.implementation_notes: Pair with local obstetric, pregnancy, and postpartum pathways; do not use pediatric-only advice when pregnancy context is present.

dispositionRules.diagnostic_target: Adolescent fever or suspected sepsis with possible active or recent pregnancy.
dispositionRules.management_change: Pregnancy or postpartum status changes escalation owner, medication choices, imaging, VTE consideration, and safety handoff.
dispositionRules.likelihood_ratio_note: Applicability routing does not have LR values; it prevents using the wrong population guideline.
dispositionRules.limitations: This is a routing rule only; use the dedicated pregnancy or postpartum pathway for detailed maternal management.

provenance.source_material: NICE NG254 suspected sepsis in under 16s, NICE NG143 fever in under 5s, Surviving Sepsis Campaign 2026 pediatric sepsis guidance, AAP 2021 febrile infant guideline, UCSF 2023 febrile infant consensus, and local module evidence rows
provenance.review_note: Pediatric fever/sepsis tree is threshold-cited and patient-traversable; local antimicrobial formulary, local sepsis alert workflow, transfer thresholds, neonatal policy, resistant-organism coverage, and obstetric pathway for pregnant/postpartum adolescents require clinician governance.

root.description: Activate for child or adolescent fever, suspected infection, hypothermia with infection concern, sepsis concern, non-blanching rash, febrile infant, source-localizing fever, or clinician-chosen pediatric fever/sepsis evaluation.

endpoint_missing_context.description: Use this endpoint when pediatric fever or suspected sepsis cannot be routed because essential age, fever, vital-sign, perfusion, source, infant-risk, treatment-safety, or disposition data are unavailable.

pediatric_fever_sepsis_bedside_assessmen.description: Initial pediatric fever/sepsis assessment requires the age-specific data needed for NICE sepsis risk grading, febrile-infant rules, source diagnosis, treatment safety, and disposition.

criteria_options.label: NICE under-5 suspected sepsis risk: SpO2 <90% high risk, SpO2 <92% moderate risk; RR and HR cutoffs are age-banded; CRT >=3 seconds, urine <1 mL/kg/h, temperature <36 C, age <3 months temperature >=38 C, or non-blanching rash are high risk
criteria_options.criteria_text: For suspected sepsis in children under 5, NICE NG254 grades high risk with SpO2 <90% in air or increased oxygen over baseline, respiratory rate >=60/min under 1 year, >=50/min age 1-2 years, >=40/min age 3-4 years, heart rate <60/min, capillary refill time >=3 seconds, catheter urine <1 mL/kg/hour, heart rate >=160/min under 1 year, >=150/min age 1-2 years, >=140/min age 3-4 years, non-blanching petechial or purpuric rash, temperature <36 C, or temperature >=38 C when younger than 3 months; moderate risk includes SpO2 <92%, RR 50-59/min under 1 year, 40-49/min age 1-2 years, 35-39/min age 3-4 years, HR 150-159/min under 1 year, 140-149/min age 1-2 years, 130-139/min age 3-4 years, age 3-6 months temperature >=39 C, leg pain, or cold hands/feet.
criteria_options.source_section: NG254 table 1 under-5 risk stratification

criteria_options.label: NICE age 5-11 suspected sepsis risk: SpO2 <90% high risk, SpO2 <92% moderate risk; RR/HR age bands, HR <60/min, CRT >=3 seconds, urine <1 mL/kg/h, tympanic temperature <36 C, or non-blanching rash change risk
criteria_options.criteria_text: For suspected sepsis age 5-11 years, NICE NG254 grades high risk with SpO2 <90% in air or increased oxygen over baseline, RR >=29/min at age 5, >=27/min age 6-7, >=25/min age 8-11, HR <60/min, CRT >=3 seconds, catheter urine <1 mL/kg/hour, HR >=130/min at age 5, >=120/min age 6-7, >=115/min age 8-11, non-blanching petechial or purpuric rash, or altered mental state; moderate risk includes SpO2 <92%, RR 24-28/min at age 5, 24-26/min age 6-7, 22-24/min age 8-11, HR 120-129/min at age 5, 110-119/min age 6-7, 105-114/min age 8-11, tympanic temperature <36 C, leg pain, or cold hands/feet.
criteria_options.source_section: NG254 table 2 age 5-11 risk stratification

criteria_options.label: NICE age 12-15 suspected sepsis risk: RR >=25/min, FiO2 >=40%, SBP <=90 mm Hg or >40 mm Hg below normal, HR >130/min, no urine 18 hours or catheter urine <0.5 mL/kg/h are high risk
criteria_options.criteria_text: For suspected sepsis age 12-15 years, NICE NG254 grades high risk with new altered mental state, RR >=25/min, need for FiO2 >=40% to maintain SpO2 >92% or >88% in known chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure, systolic blood pressure <=90 mm Hg or >40 mm Hg below normal, heart rate >130/min, no urine in the previous 18 hours, catheter urine <0.5 mL/kg/hour, or non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash; moderate risk includes acute deterioration, impaired immunity, trauma/surgery/invasive procedure in the last 6 weeks, RR 21-24/min, SBP 91-100 mm Hg, HR 91-130/min or 100-130/min in pregnancy, new arrhythmia, no urine 12-18 hours, catheter urine 0.5-1 mL/kg/hour, tympanic temperature <36 C, or surgical-site infection signs.
cutoffs: SpO2 >88% in chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure
data_needed: systolic blood pressure and age-normal baseline
criteria_options.source_section: NG254 table 3 age 12-15 risk stratification

criteria_options.label: NICE pediatric sepsis initial therapy: ceftriaxone 80 mg/kg daily maximum 4 g if not newborn; newborn ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily only if >40 weeks corrected GA and no IV calcium; fluid bolus 10 mL/kg under 16 maximum 250 mL over <10 minutes
criteria_options.criteria_text: NICE NG254 recommends local antimicrobial guidance when the infection source is clear; for suspected community-acquired sepsis under 16 excluding newborns, ceftriaxone 80 mg/kg once daily to maximum 4 g/day; for newborn community-acquired sepsis, ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily only when corrected gestational age is >40 weeks and no IV calcium is running, otherwise cefotaxime 50 mg/kg every 6-12 hours; under 3 months add listeria-active ampicillin or amoxicillin; newborn fluid bolus is 10-20 mL/kg over <10 minutes and under-16 bolus is 10 mL/kg over <10 minutes maximum 250 mL, with smaller volumes for cardiac or kidney disease and consultant in-person attendance if no improvement after a second bolus.
data_needed: response after first and second bolus
criteria_options.source_section: NG254 antibiotic, fluid, and oxygen recommendations 1.8-1.10

criteria_options.label: SSC pediatric sepsis timing and fluids: blood cultures before antibiotics if no substantial delay, antibiotics ideally within 1 hour for septic shock and within 3 hours for probable sepsis without shock; fluid boluses 10-20 mL/kg with repeated reassessment
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC pediatric guidance recommends lactate for probable sepsis or suspected septic shock, blood cultures before antibiotics if this does not substantially delay therapy, antibiotics ideally within 1 hour for suspected septic shock and within 3 hours for probable sepsis without shock, and repeated reassessment after each 10-20 mL/kg bolus; where ICU is available, give up to 40-60 mL/kg in the first hour, while without ICU do not bolus sepsis without hypotension and give up to 40 mL/kg in the first hour for septic shock with hypotension; stop boluses when shock resolves or fluid overload develops, consider peripheral vasoactives rather than delaying for central access, and target SpO2 88-92% after resuscitation.
cutoffs: 40 mL/kg first hour without ICU if hypotensive shock
data_needed: central venous access and ScvO2 if available
criteria_options.source_section: SSC 2026 pediatric infection, fluids, vasoactive, and ventilation recommendations

criteria_options.label: Febrile infant inflammatory markers: fever >38.0 C is true fever; elevated inflammatory markers include ANC >4000/uL, CRP >20 mg/L or 2.0 mg/dL, procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL, or temperature >38.5 C
criteria_options.criteria_text: UCSF febrile infant consensus uses temperature >38.0 C as true fever and inflammatory marker cutoffs of ANC >4000/uL, CRP >20 mg/L or 2.0 mg/dL, procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL, or temperature >38.5 C; PECARN low risk is UA negative, ANC <4000/uL, and procalcitonin <0.5 ng/mL.
criteria_options.source_section: Febrile infant inflammatory marker and PECARN low-risk criteria

criteria_options.label: Febrile infant age branches: 0-21 days hospitalize with blood, urine, CSF cultures and empiric antibiotics 24-48 hours; 22-28 days and 29-60 days route by UA, inflammatory markers, CSF, and 24-hour follow-up reliability
criteria_options.criteria_text: UCSF consensus recommends all well-appearing febrile infants under 21 days receive blood, urine, and CSF analysis/cultures and hospitalization with empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for 24-48 hours while cultures are pending; age 22-28 days needs blood culture, inflammatory markers, UA/urine culture if positive, CSF and admission/IV antibiotics when markers are elevated or UA/CSF high risk, and selected low-risk outpatient observation only with reliable follow-up; age 29-60 days needs UA, urine culture if positive, blood culture, inflammatory markers, and may avoid CSF and use home oral UTI therapy or observation when low-risk criteria and follow-up/transport/communication are met.
data_needed: bronchiolitis or focal bacterial infection exclusion
criteria_options.source_section: Febrile infant age-based evaluation, admission, discharge, and antibiotic discontinuation criteria

criteria_options.label: NICE fever under 5 infant testing: under 3 months measure temperature/HR/RR, obtain FBC, blood culture, CRP, urine test; LP and parenteral antibiotics for age <1 month, unwell age 1-3 months, or WBC <5 or >15 x10^9/L
criteria_options.criteria_text: NICE NG143 recommends infants under 3 months with fever have temperature, heart rate, and respiratory rate recorded plus full blood count, blood culture, CRP, urine test, CXR only with respiratory signs, stool culture if diarrhoea; lumbar puncture unless contraindicated and parenteral antibiotics are indicated for infants younger than 1 month, all unwell infants 1-3 months, or infants 1-3 months with WBC <5 x10^9/L or >15 x10^9/L; when parenteral antibiotics are used in infants under 3 months, use a third-generation cephalosporin plus listeria-active ampicillin or amoxicillin.
criteria_options.source_section: NG143 pediatric specialist management of infants younger than 3 months

criteria_options.label: NICE fever under 5 safety-net: red features urgent pediatric specialist, amber features safety-net if no diagnosis, fever >=5 days or non-blanching rash/fit/worsening concern requires further help; antipyretics only for distress
criteria_options.criteria_text: NICE NG143 recommends emergency care for immediately life-threatening fever features, urgent pediatric specialist care for red features, safety-net or specialist assessment for amber features with no diagnosis, and home care only for green features; if not admitted with red or amber features and no diagnosis, provide warning symptoms, planned follow-up, and direct access; antipyretics are for distress only, not just temperature reduction, and paracetamol and ibuprofen should not be given simultaneously; seek further help for fit, non-blanching rash, less well, increasing carer worry, fever lasting 5 days or longer, or carer inability to cope.
criteria_options.source_section: NG143 non-paediatric and paediatric management, antipyretics, and home-care safety-netting

pediatric_fever_sepsis_classification_de.description: Choose the pediatric fever/sepsis branch from shock, high-risk sepsis, moderate-risk sepsis, febrile infant, meningococcal/CNS/HSV, source-directed fever, pregnancy/postpartum, special-population review, low-risk safety-net, or monitoring.

pediatric_fever_sepsis_missing_objective.description: Use this endpoint when branch selection depends on objective values that are absent, including NICE age-banded vital thresholds, SSC shock data, or febrile-infant inflammatory markers and cultures.

pediatric_fever_sepsis_septic_shock_endp.description: Use when suspected infection has shock, life-threatening organ dysfunction, severe hypoperfusion, or immediate pediatric critical-care need.

criteria_options.criteria_text: For suspected sepsis age 12-15 years, NICE NG254 grades high risk with new altered mental state, RR >=25/min, need for FiO2 >=40% to maintain SpO2 >92% or >88% in known chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure, systolic blood pressure <=90 mm Hg or >40 mm Hg below normal, heart rate >130/min, no urine in the previous 18 hours, catheter urine <0.5 mL/kg/hour, or non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash; moderate risk includes acute deterioration, impaired immunity, trauma/surgery/invasive procedure in the last 6 weeks, RR 21-24/min, SBP 91-100 mm Hg, HR 91-130/min or 100-130/min in pregnancy, new arrhythmia, no urine 12-18 hours, catheter urine 0.5-1 mL/kg/hour, tympanic temperature <36 C, or surgical-site infection signs.
cutoffs: SpO2 >88% in chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure
data_needed: systolic blood pressure and age-normal baseline
criteria_options.source_section: NG254 table 3 age 12-15 risk stratification

criteria_options.label: NICE pediatric sepsis initial therapy: ceftriaxone 80 mg/kg daily maximum 4 g if not newborn; newborn ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily only if >40 weeks corrected GA and no IV calcium; fluid bolus 10 mL/kg under 16 maximum 250 mL over <10 minutes
criteria_options.criteria_text: NICE NG254 recommends local antimicrobial guidance when the infection source is clear; for suspected community-acquired sepsis under 16 excluding newborns, ceftriaxone 80 mg/kg once daily to maximum 4 g/day; for newborn community-acquired sepsis, ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily only when corrected gestational age is >40 weeks and no IV calcium is running, otherwise cefotaxime 50 mg/kg every 6-12 hours; under 3 months add listeria-active ampicillin or amoxicillin; newborn fluid bolus is 10-20 mL/kg over <10 minutes and under-16 bolus is 10 mL/kg over <10 minutes maximum 250 mL, with smaller volumes for cardiac or kidney disease and consultant in-person attendance if no improvement after a second bolus.
data_needed: response after first and second bolus
criteria_options.source_section: NG254 antibiotic, fluid, and oxygen recommendations 1.8-1.10

criteria_options.label: SSC pediatric sepsis timing and fluids: blood cultures before antibiotics if no substantial delay, antibiotics ideally within 1 hour for septic shock and within 3 hours for probable sepsis without shock; fluid boluses 10-20 mL/kg with repeated reassessment
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC pediatric guidance recommends lactate for probable sepsis or suspected septic shock, blood cultures before antibiotics if this does not substantially delay therapy, antibiotics ideally within 1 hour for suspected septic shock and within 3 hours for probable sepsis without shock, and repeated reassessment after each 10-20 mL/kg bolus; where ICU is available, give up to 40-60 mL/kg in the first hour, while without ICU do not bolus sepsis without hypotension and give up to 40 mL/kg in the first hour for septic shock with hypotension; stop boluses when shock resolves or fluid overload develops, consider peripheral vasoactives rather than delaying for central access, and target SpO2 88-92% after resuscitation.
cutoffs: 40 mL/kg first hour without ICU if hypotensive shock
data_needed: central venous access and ScvO2 if available
criteria_options.source_section: SSC 2026 pediatric infection, fluids, vasoactive, and ventilation recommendations

criteria_options.label: Febrile infant inflammatory markers: fever >38.0 C is true fever; elevated inflammatory markers include ANC >4000/uL, CRP >20 mg/L or 2.0 mg/dL, procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL, or temperature >38.5 C
criteria_options.criteria_text: UCSF febrile infant consensus uses temperature >38.0 C as true fever and inflammatory marker cutoffs of ANC >4000/uL, CRP >20 mg/L or 2.0 mg/dL, procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL, or temperature >38.5 C; PECARN low risk is UA negative, ANC <4000/uL, and procalcitonin <0.5 ng/mL.
criteria_options.source_section: Febrile infant inflammatory marker and PECARN low-risk criteria

criteria_options.label: Febrile infant age branches: 0-21 days hospitalize with blood, urine, CSF cultures and empiric antibiotics 24-48 hours; 22-28 days and 29-60 days route by UA, inflammatory markers, CSF, and 24-hour follow-up reliability
criteria_options.criteria_text: UCSF consensus recommends all well-appearing febrile infants under 21 days receive blood, urine, and CSF analysis/cultures and hospitalization with empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for 24-48 hours while cultures are pending; age 22-28 days needs blood culture, inflammatory markers, UA/urine culture if positive, CSF and admission/IV antibiotics when markers are elevated or UA/CSF high risk, and selected low-risk outpatient observation only with reliable follow-up; age 29-60 days needs UA, urine culture if positive, blood culture, inflammatory markers, and may avoid CSF and use home oral UTI therapy or observation when low-risk criteria and follow-up/transport/communication are met.
data_needed: bronchiolitis or focal bacterial infection exclusion
criteria_options.source_section: Febrile infant age-based evaluation, admission, discharge, and antibiotic discontinuation criteria

criteria_options.label: NICE fever under 5 infant testing: under 3 months measure temperature/HR/RR, obtain FBC, blood culture, CRP, urine test; LP and parenteral antibiotics for age <1 month, unwell age 1-3 months, or WBC <5 or >15 x10^9/L
criteria_options.criteria_text: NICE NG143 recommends infants under 3 months with fever have temperature, heart rate, and respiratory rate recorded plus full blood count, blood culture, CRP, urine test, CXR only with respiratory signs, stool culture if diarrhoea; lumbar puncture unless contraindicated and parenteral antibiotics are indicated for infants younger than 1 month, all unwell infants 1-3 months, or infants 1-3 months with WBC <5 x10^9/L or >15 x10^9/L; when parenteral antibiotics are used in infants under 3 months, use a third-generation cephalosporin plus listeria-active ampicillin or amoxicillin.
criteria_options.source_section: NG143 pediatric specialist management of infants younger than 3 months

criteria_options.label: NICE fever under 5 safety-net: red features urgent pediatric specialist, amber features safety-net if no diagnosis, fever >=5 days or non-blanching rash/fit/worsening concern requires further help; antipyretics only for distress
criteria_options.criteria_text: NICE NG143 recommends emergency care for immediately life-threatening fever features, urgent pediatric specialist care for red features, safety-net or specialist assessment for amber features with no diagnosis, and home care only for green features; if not admitted with red or amber features and no diagnosis, provide warning symptoms, planned follow-up, and direct access; antipyretics are for distress only, not just temperature reduction, and paracetamol and ibuprofen should not be given simultaneously; seek further help for fit, non-blanching rash, less well, increasing carer worry, fever lasting 5 days or longer, or carer inability to cope.
criteria_options.source_section: NG143 non-paediatric and paediatric management, antipyretics, and home-care safety-netting

pediatric_fever_sepsis_nice_high_risk_en.description: Use when suspected sepsis meets any NICE NG254 high-risk criterion but the shock endpoint is not already active.

criteria_options.label: NICE under-5 suspected sepsis risk: SpO2 <90% high risk, SpO2 <92% moderate risk; RR and HR cutoffs are age-banded; CRT >=3 seconds, urine <1 mL/kg/h, temperature <36 C, age <3 months temperature >=38 C, or non-blanching rash are high risk
criteria_options.criteria_text: For suspected sepsis in children under 5, NICE NG254 grades high risk with SpO2 <90% in air or increased oxygen over baseline, respiratory rate >=60/min under 1 year, >=50/min age 1-2 years, >=40/min age 3-4 years, heart rate <60/min, capillary refill time >=3 seconds, catheter urine <1 mL/kg/hour, heart rate >=160/min under 1 year, >=150/min age 1-2 years, >=140/min age 3-4 years, non-blanching petechial or purpuric rash, temperature <36 C, or temperature >=38 C when younger than 3 months; moderate risk includes SpO2 <92%, RR 50-59/min under 1 year, 40-49/min age 1-2 years, 35-39/min age 3-4 years, HR 150-159/min under 1 year, 140-149/min age 1-2 years, 130-139/min age 3-4 years, age 3-6 months temperature >=39 C, leg pain, or cold hands/feet.

criteria_options.label: NICE age 5-11 suspected sepsis risk: SpO2 <90% high risk, SpO2 <92% moderate risk; RR/HR age bands, HR <60/min, CRT >=3 seconds, urine <1 mL/kg/h, tympanic temperature <36 C, or non-blanching rash change risk

criteria_options.label: NICE pediatric sepsis initial therapy: ceftriaxone 80 mg/kg daily maximum 4 g if not newborn; newborn ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily only if >40 weeks corrected GA and no IV calcium; fluid bolus 10 mL/kg under 16 maximum 250 mL over <10 minutes
criteria_options.criteria_text: NICE NG254 recommends local antimicrobial guidance when the infection source is clear; for suspected community-acquired sepsis under 16 excluding newborns, ceftriaxone 80 mg/kg once daily to maximum 4 g/day; for newborn community-acquired sepsis, ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily only when corrected gestational age is >40 weeks and no IV calcium is running, otherwise cefotaxime 50 mg/kg every 6-12 hours; under 3 months add listeria-active ampicillin or amoxicillin; newborn fluid bolus is 10-20 mL/kg over <10 minutes and under-16 bolus is 10 mL/kg over <10 minutes maximum 250 mL, with smaller volumes for cardiac or kidney disease and consultant in-person attendance if no improvement after a second bolus.
data_needed: response after first and second bolus
criteria_options.source_section: NG254 antibiotic, fluid, and oxygen recommendations 1.8-1.10

criteria_options.label: SSC pediatric sepsis timing and fluids: blood cultures before antibiotics if no substantial delay, antibiotics ideally within 1 hour for septic shock and within 3 hours for probable sepsis without shock; fluid boluses 10-20 mL/kg with repeated reassessment
criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC pediatric guidance recommends lactate for probable sepsis or suspected septic shock, blood cultures before antibiotics if this does not substantially delay therapy, antibiotics ideally within 1 hour for suspected septic shock and within 3 hours for probable sepsis without shock, and repeated reassessment after each 10-20 mL/kg bolus; where ICU is available, give up to 40-60 mL/kg in the first hour, while without ICU do not bolus sepsis without hypotension and give up to 40 mL/kg in the first hour for septic shock with hypotension; stop boluses when shock resolves or fluid overload develops, consider peripheral vasoactives rather than delaying for central access, and target SpO2 88-92% after resuscitation.
cutoffs: 40 mL/kg first hour without ICU if hypotensive shock
data_needed: central venous access and ScvO2 if available
criteria_options.source_section: SSC 2026 pediatric infection, fluids, vasoactive, and ventilation recommendations

criteria_options.label: Febrile infant inflammatory markers: fever >38.0 C is true fever; elevated inflammatory markers include ANC >4000/uL, CRP >20 mg/L or 2.0 mg/dL, procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL, or temperature >38.5 C
criteria_options.criteria_text: UCSF febrile infant consensus uses temperature >38.0 C as true fever and inflammatory marker cutoffs of ANC >4000/uL, CRP >20 mg/L or 2.0 mg/dL, procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL, or temperature >38.5 C; PECARN low risk is UA negative, ANC <4000/uL, and procalcitonin <0.5 ng/mL.
criteria_options.source_section: Febrile infant inflammatory marker and PECARN low-risk criteria

criteria_options.label: Febrile infant age branches: 0-21 days hospitalize with blood, urine, CSF cultures and empiric antibiotics 24-48 hours; 22-28 days and 29-60 days route by UA, inflammatory markers, CSF, and 24-hour follow-up reliability
criteria_options.criteria_text: UCSF consensus recommends all well-appearing febrile infants under 21 days receive blood, urine, and CSF analysis/cultures and hospitalization with empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for 24-48 hours while cultures are pending; age 22-28 days needs blood culture, inflammatory markers, UA/urine culture if positive, CSF and admission/IV antibiotics when markers are elevated or UA/CSF high risk, and selected low-risk outpatient observation only with reliable follow-up; age 29-60 days needs UA, urine culture if positive, blood culture, inflammatory markers, and may avoid CSF and use home oral UTI therapy or observation when low-risk criteria and follow-up/transport/communication are met.
data_needed: bronchiolitis or focal bacterial infection exclusion
criteria_options.source_section: Febrile infant age-based evaluation, admission, discharge, and antibiotic discontinuation criteria

criteria_options.label: NICE fever under 5 infant testing: under 3 months measure temperature/HR/RR, obtain FBC, blood culture, CRP, urine test; LP and parenteral antibiotics for age <1 month, unwell age 1-3 months, or WBC <5 or >15 x10^9/L
criteria_options.criteria_text: NICE NG143 recommends infants under 3 months with fever have temperature, heart rate, and respiratory rate recorded plus full blood count, blood culture, CRP, urine test, CXR only with respiratory signs, stool culture if diarrhoea; lumbar puncture unless contraindicated and parenteral antibiotics are indicated for infants younger than 1 month, all unwell infants 1-3 months, or infants 1-3 months with WBC <5 x10^9/L or >15 x10^9/L; when parenteral antibiotics are used in infants under 3 months, use a third-generation cephalosporin plus listeria-active ampicillin or amoxicillin.
criteria_options.source_section: NG143 pediatric specialist management of infants younger than 3 months

criteria_options.label: NICE fever under 5 safety-net: red features urgent pediatric specialist, amber features safety-net if no diagnosis, fever >=5 days or non-blanching rash/fit/worsening concern requires further help; antipyretics only for distress
criteria_options.criteria_text: NICE NG143 recommends emergency care for immediately life-threatening fever features, urgent pediatric specialist care for red features, safety-net or specialist assessment for amber features with no diagnosis, and home care only for green features; if not admitted with red or amber features and no diagnosis, provide warning symptoms, planned follow-up, and direct access; antipyretics are for distress only, not just temperature reduction, and paracetamol and ibuprofen should not be given simultaneously; seek further help for fit, non-blanching rash, less well, increasing carer worry, fever lasting 5 days or longer, or carer inability to cope.
criteria_options.source_section: NG143 non-paediatric and paediatric management, antipyretics, and home-care safety-netting

pediatric_fever_sepsis_meningococcal_cns.description: Use when fever has meningococcal disease, meningitis, encephalitis, or HSV features that override routine fever management.

criteria_options.label: NICE under-5 suspected sepsis risk: SpO2 <90% high risk, SpO2 <92% moderate risk; RR and HR cutoffs are age-banded; CRT >=3 seconds, urine <1 mL/kg/h, temperature <36 C, age <3 months temperature >=38 C, or non-blanching rash are high risk
criteria_options.criteria_text: For suspected sepsis in children under 5, NICE NG254 grades high risk with SpO2 <90% in air or increased oxygen over baseline, respiratory rate >=60/min under 1 year, >=50/min age 1-2 years, >=40/min age 3-4 years, heart rate <60/min, capillary refill time >=3 seconds, catheter urine <1 mL/kg/hour, heart rate >=160/min under 1 year, >=150/min age 1-2 years, >=140/min age 3-4 years, non-blanching petechial or purpuric rash, temperature <36 C, or temperature >=38 C when younger than 3 months; moderate risk includes SpO2 <92%, RR 50-59/min under 1 year, 40-49/min age 1-2 years, 35-39/min age 3-4 years, HR 150-159/min under 1 year, 140-149/min age 1-2 years, 130-139/min age 3-4 years, age 3-6 months temperature >=39 C, leg pain, or cold hands/feet.
criteria_options.source_section: NG254 table 1 under-5 risk stratification

criteria_options.label: NICE age 5-11 suspected sepsis risk: SpO2 <90% high risk, SpO2 <92% moderate risk; RR/HR age bands, HR <60/min, CRT >=3 seconds, urine <1 mL/kg/h, tympanic temperature <36 C, or non-blanching rash change risk
criteria_options.criteria_text: For suspected sepsis age 5-11 years, NICE NG254 grades high risk with SpO2 <90% in air or increased oxygen over baseline, RR >=29/min at age 5, >=27/min age 6-7, >=25/min age 8-11, HR <60/min, CRT >=3 seconds, catheter urine <1 mL/kg/hour, HR >=130/min at age 5, >=120/min age 6-7, >=115/min age 8-11, non-blanching petechial or purpuric rash, or altered mental state; moderate risk includes SpO2 <92%, RR 24-28/min at age 5, 24-26/min age 6-7, 22-24/min age 8-11, HR 120-129/min at age 5, 110-119/min age 6-7, 105-114/min age 8-11, tympanic temperature <36 C, leg pain, or cold hands/feet.
criteria_options.source_section: NG254 table 2 age 5-11 risk stratification

criteria_options.label: NICE age 12-15 suspected sepsis risk: RR >=25/min, FiO2 >=40%, SBP <=90 mm Hg or >40 mm Hg below normal, HR >130/min, no urine 18 hours or catheter urine <0.5 mL/kg/h are high risk
criteria_options.criteria_text: For suspected sepsis age 12-15 years, NICE NG254 grades high risk with new altered mental state, RR >=25/min, need for FiO2 >=40% to maintain SpO2 >92% or >88% in known chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure, systolic blood pressure <=90 mm Hg or >40 mm Hg below normal, heart rate >130/min, no urine in the previous 18 hours, catheter urine <0.5 mL/kg/hour, or non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash; moderate risk includes acute deterioration, impaired immunity, trauma/surgery/invasive procedure in the last 6 weeks, RR 21-24/min, SBP 91-100 mm Hg, HR 91-130/min or 100-130/min in pregnancy, new arrhythmia, no urine 12-18 hours, catheter urine 0.5-1 mL/kg/hour, tympanic temperature <36 C, or surgical-site infection signs.
cutoffs: SpO2 >88% in chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure
data_needed: systolic blood pressure and age-normal baseline
criteria_options.source_section: NG254 table 3 age 12-15 risk stratification

criteria_options.criteria_text: SSC pediatric guidance recommends lactate for probable sepsis or suspected septic shock, blood cultures before antibiotics if this does not substantially delay therapy, antibiotics ideally within 1 hour for suspected septic shock and within 3 hours for probable sepsis without shock, and repeated reassessment after each 10-20 mL/kg bolus; where ICU is available, give up to 40-60 mL/kg in the first hour, while without ICU do not bolus sepsis without hypotension and give up to 40 mL/kg in the first hour for septic shock with hypotension; stop boluses when shock resolves or fluid overload develops, consider peripheral vasoactives rather than delaying for central access, and target SpO2 88-92% after resuscitation.
cutoffs: 40 mL/kg first hour without ICU if hypotensive shock
data_needed: central venous access and ScvO2 if available
criteria_options.source_section: SSC 2026 pediatric infection, fluids, vasoactive, and ventilation recommendations

criteria_options.label: Febrile infant inflammatory markers: fever >38.0 C is true fever; elevated inflammatory markers include ANC >4000/uL, CRP >20 mg/L or 2.0 mg/dL, procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL, or temperature >38.5 C
criteria_options.criteria_text: UCSF febrile infant consensus uses temperature >38.0 C as true fever and inflammatory marker cutoffs of ANC >4000/uL, CRP >20 mg/L or 2.0 mg/dL, procalcitonin >0.5 ng/mL, or temperature >38.5 C; PECARN low risk is UA negative, ANC <4000/uL, and procalcitonin <0.5 ng/mL.
criteria_options.source_section: Febrile infant inflammatory marker and PECARN low-risk criteria

criteria_options.label: Febrile infant age branches: 0-21 days hospitalize with blood, urine, CSF cultures and empiric antibiotics 24-48 hours; 22-28 days and 29-60 days route by UA, inflammatory markers, CSF, and 24-hour follow-up reliability
criteria_options.criteria_text: UCSF consensus recommends all well-appearing febrile infants under 21 days receive blood, urine, and CSF analysis/cultures and hospitalization with empiric broad-spectrum antibiotics for 24-48 hours while cultures are pending; age 22-28 days needs blood culture, inflammatory markers, UA/urine culture if positive, CSF and admission/IV antibiotics when markers are elevated or UA/CSF high risk, and selected low-risk outpatient observation only with reliable follow-up; age 29-60 days needs UA, urine culture if positive, blood culture, inflammatory markers, and may avoid CSF and use home oral UTI therapy or observation when low-risk criteria and follow-up/transport/communication are met.
data_needed: bronchiolitis or focal bacterial infection exclusion
criteria_options.source_section: Febrile infant age-based evaluation, admission, discharge, and antibiotic discontinuation criteria

criteria_options.label: NICE fever under 5 infant testing: under 3 months measure temperature/HR/RR, obtain FBC, blood culture, CRP, urine test; LP and parenteral antibiotics for age <1 month, unwell age 1-3 months, or WBC <5 or >15 x10^9/L
criteria_options.criteria_text: NICE NG143 recommends infants under 3 months with fever have temperature, heart rate, and respiratory rate recorded plus full blood count, blood culture, CRP, urine test, CXR only with respiratory signs, stool culture if diarrhoea; lumbar puncture unless contraindicated and parenteral antibiotics are indicated for infants younger than 1 month, all unwell infants 1-3 months, or infants 1-3 months with WBC <5 x10^9/L or >15 x10^9/L; when parenteral antibiotics are used in infants under 3 months, use a third-generation cephalosporin plus listeria-active ampicillin or amoxicillin.
criteria_options.source_section: NG143 pediatric specialist management of infants younger than 3 months

criteria_options.label: NICE fever under 5 safety-net: red features urgent pediatric specialist, amber features safety-net if no diagnosis, fever >=5 days or non-blanching rash/fit/worsening concern requires further help; antipyretics only for distress
criteria_options.criteria_text: NICE NG143 recommends emergency care for immediately life-threatening fever features, urgent pediatric specialist care for red features, safety-net or specialist assessment for amber features with no diagnosis, and home care only for green features; if not admitted with red or amber features and no diagnosis, provide warning symptoms, planned follow-up, and direct access; antipyretics are for distress only, not just temperature reduction, and paracetamol and ibuprofen should not be given simultaneously; seek further help for fit, non-blanching rash, less well, increasing carer worry, fever lasting 5 days or longer, or carer inability to cope.
criteria_options.source_section: NG143 non-paediatric and paediatric management, antipyretics, and home-care safety-netting

pediatric_fever_sepsis_special_populatio.description: Use when host factors, resistant-organism risk, febrile-infant exclusions, antimicrobial contraindications, or local pediatric transfer/formulary policies change routine fever management.

criteria_options.label: NICE under-5 suspected sepsis risk: SpO2 <90% high risk, SpO2 <92% moderate risk; RR and HR cutoffs are age-banded; CRT >=3 seconds, urine <1 mL/kg/h, temperature <36 C, age <3 months temperature >=38 C, or non-blanching rash are high risk
criteria_options.criteria_text: For suspected sepsis in children under 5, NICE NG254 grades high risk with SpO2 <90% in air or increased oxygen over baseline, respiratory rate >=60/min under 1 year, >=50/min age 1-2 years, >=40/min age 3-4 years, heart rate <60/min, capillary refill time >=3 seconds, catheter urine <1 mL/kg/hour, heart rate >=160/min under 1 year, >=150/min age 1-2 years, >=140/min age 3-4 years, non-blanching petechial or purpuric rash, temperature <36 C, or temperature >=38 C when younger than 3 months; moderate risk includes SpO2 <92%, RR 50-59/min under 1 year, 40-49/min age 1-2 years, 35-39/min age 3-4 years, HR 150-159/min under 1 year, 140-149/min age 1-2 years, 130-139/min age 3-4 years, age 3-6 months temperature >=39 C, leg pain, or cold hands/feet.
criteria_options.source_section: NG254 table 1 under-5 risk stratification

criteria_options.label: NICE age 5-11 suspected sepsis risk: SpO2 <90% high risk, SpO2 <92% moderate risk; RR/HR age bands, HR <60/min, CRT >=3 seconds, urine <1 mL/kg/h, tympanic temperature <36 C, or non-blanching rash change risk
criteria_options.criteria_text: For suspected sepsis age 5-11 years, NICE NG254 grades high risk with SpO2 <90% in air or increased oxygen over baseline, RR >=29/min at age 5, >=27/min age 6-7, >=25/min age 8-11, HR <60/min, CRT >=3 seconds, catheter urine <1 mL/kg/hour, HR >=130/min at age 5, >=120/min age 6-7, >=115/min age 8-11, non-blanching petechial or purpuric rash, or altered mental state; moderate risk includes SpO2 <92%, RR 24-28/min at age 5, 24-26/min age 6-7, 22-24/min age 8-11, HR 120-129/min at age 5, 110-119/min age 6-7, 105-114/min age 8-11, tympanic temperature <36 C, leg pain, or cold hands/feet.
criteria_options.source_section: NG254 table 2 age 5-11 risk stratification

criteria_options.label: NICE age 12-15 suspected sepsis risk: RR >=25/min, FiO2 >=40%, SBP <=90 mm Hg or >40 mm Hg below normal, HR >130/min, no urine 18 hours or catheter urine <0.5 mL/kg/h are high risk
criteria_options.criteria_text: For suspected sepsis age 12-15 years, NICE NG254 grades high risk with new altered mental state, RR >=25/min, need for FiO2 >=40% to maintain SpO2 >92% or >88% in known chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure, systolic blood pressure <=90 mm Hg or >40 mm Hg below normal, heart rate >130/min, no urine in the previous 18 hours, catheter urine <0.5 mL/kg/hour, or non-blanching petechial/purpuric rash; moderate risk includes acute deterioration, impaired immunity, trauma/surgery/invasive procedure in the last 6 weeks, RR 21-24/min, SBP 91-100 mm Hg, HR 91-130/min or 100-130/min in pregnancy, new arrhythmia, no urine 12-18 hours, catheter urine 0.5-1 mL/kg/hour, tympanic temperature <36 C, or surgical-site infection signs.
cutoffs: SpO2 >88% in chronic hypercapnic respiratory failure
data_needed: systolic blood pressure and age-normal baseline
criteria_options.source_section: NG254 table 3 age 12-15 risk stratification

criteria_options.label: NICE pediatric sepsis initial therapy: ceftriaxone 80 mg/kg daily maximum 4 g if not newborn; newborn ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg daily only if >40 weeks corrected GA and no IV calcium; fluid bolus 10 mL/kg under 16 maximum 250 mL over <10 minutes
criteria_options.criteria_text: NICE NG254 recommends local antimicrobial guidance when the infection source is clear; for suspected community-acquired sepsis under 16 excluding newborns, ceftriaxone 80 mg/kg once daily to maximum 4 g/day; for newborn community-acquired sepsis, ceftriaxone 50 mg/kg once daily only when corrected gestational age is >40 weeks and no IV calcium is running, otherwise cefotaxime 50 mg/kg every 6-12 hours; under 3 months add listeria-active ampicillin or amoxicillin; newborn fluid bolus is 10-20 mL/kg over <10 minutes and under-16 bolus is 10 mL/kg over <10 minutes maximum 250 mL, with smaller volumes for cardiac or kidney disease and consultant in-person attendance if no improvement after a second bolus.
data_needed: response after first and second bolus
criteria_options.source_section: NG254 antibiotic, fluid, and oxygen recommendations 1.8-1.10
